intervention: Practice 4-7-8 breathing: inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat 3 times.
evidence: Activates the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing cortisol and heart rate.
source: Weil, A. (2015). Spontaneous Happiness. Hachette Books.

intervention: Try box breathing: inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s. Used by military personnel for acute stress.
evidence: Reduces sympathetic nervous system activation and lowers EDA response.
source: Navy SEAL stress management protocol.

intervention: Take a 5-minute break. Step away from your screen, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds.
evidence: Micro-breaks reduce cognitive fatigue and restore attentional resources (Trougakos & Hideg, 2009).
source: Trougakos, J. P., & Hideg, I. (2009). Momentary work recovery.

intervention: Do a 2-minute progressive muscle relaxation: tense and release your shoulders, arms, and hands.
evidence: PMR reduces musculoskeletal tension and lowers heart rate variability stress markers.
source: Jacobson, E. (1938). Progressive Relaxation. University of Chicago Press.

intervention: Grip the steering wheel less tightly. Roll your shoulders back and take a slow, deep breath.
evidence: Physical tension reduction during driving lowers reported stress and improves reaction time.
source: Healey & Picard (2005). Detecting stress during real-world driving.

intervention: If safe to do so, slow down your driving speed slightly. A 10% speed reduction reduces stress arousal.
evidence: Speed and stress are positively correlated in real-world driving studies.
source: AffectiveROAD study, El Haouij et al. (2018).

intervention: Try a 5-4-3-2-1 grounding exercise: notice 5 things you can see, 4 you can touch, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, 1 you can taste.
evidence: Grounding techniques redirect attention away from stressors and reduce anxiety (Borkovec et al., 2002).
source: Borkovec, T. D., et al. (2002). Cognitive-behavioral therapy for generalized anxiety.

intervention: Drink a glass of water slowly. Dehydration amplifies physiological stress responses.
evidence: Even mild dehydration (1-2%) increases cortisol and perceived stress.
source: Masento et al. (2014). Effects of hydration status on cognitive performance.

intervention: Stand up and do 10 slow shoulder rolls. Physical movement interrupts the stress-tension cycle.
evidence: Brief movement breaks reduce muscle tension and improve mood (Thayer, 1996).
source: Thayer, R. E. (1996). The Origin of Everyday Moods.

intervention: Write down the ONE most urgent task and set a 25-minute focused timer. Clarity reduces cognitive overwhelm.
evidence: The Pomodoro technique reduces task-related stress and increases productivity.
source: Cirillo, F. (2006). The Pomodoro Technique.

intervention: Silently repeat a calming phrase to yourself: 'This moment will pass. I am capable.'
evidence: Positive self-talk reduces emotional reactivity and stress during challenging tasks.
source: Hatzigeorgiadis et al. (2011). Self-talk and sports performance.

intervention: Try a body scan: close your eyes for 60 seconds and mentally scan from your feet to your head, noting tension.
evidence: Body scan meditation activates the parasympathetic nervous system (Kabat-Zinn, 1990).
source: Kabat-Zinn, J. (1990). Full Catastrophe Living. Dell Publishing.

intervention: Listen to 2 minutes of calm music (60-80 BPM). Music at resting heart rate can synchronize physiological rhythms.
evidence: Music therapy reduces cortisol and promotes parasympathetic activation.
source: Thoma et al. (2013). The effect of music on the human stress response.

intervention: Reconsider your current priority. Ask: 'What is truly urgent vs. what feels urgent?'
evidence: Cognitive reappraisal of stressors reduces physiological arousal (Gross, 1998).
source: Gross, J. J. (1998). Antecedent and response-focused emotion regulation.

intervention: Take a 10-minute walk outside, even around the building. Natural light and movement significantly reduce cortisol.
evidence: Outdoor walking in natural settings reduces cortisol by up to 16% (Park et al., 2010).
source: Park, B. J., et al. (2010). The physiological effects of Shinrin-yoku.

intervention: Diaphragmatic breathing: breathe into your belly, not your chest. 6 breaths per minute for 2 minutes.
evidence: Resonance frequency breathing (6 breaths/min) maximizes HRV and reduces sympathetic tone.
source: Lehrer & Gevirtz (2014). Heart rate variability biofeedback.

intervention: Put on your parking brake at the next opportunity and stretch your neck gently side to side.
evidence: Neck and shoulder tension is a known correlate of driving stress.
source: Cardiovascular effects of driving stress research.

intervention: Focus on one email or task at a time. Close other tabs. Multitasking increases cortisol by 15%.
evidence: Task-switching and interruptions increase stress and reduce performance.
source: SWELL-KW study (Koldijk et al., 2014).

intervention: Acknowledge the stressor out loud or in writing: 'I am stressed because ___.' Labeling reduces amygdala activation.
evidence: Affect labeling reduces neural stress responses (Lieberman et al., 2007).
source: Lieberman, M. D., et al. (2007). Putting feelings into words.

intervention: Set a realistic expectation for the next 30 minutes. Lower the bar slightly — done is better than perfect.
evidence: Perfectionism is a significant predictor of work-related stress.
source: Flett & Hewitt (2002). Perfectionism and maladjustment.

intervention: Try alternate nostril breathing: close right nostril, inhale left 4s, switch, exhale right 4s. Repeat 5 cycles.
evidence: Nadi Shodhana pranayama reduces sympathetic activity and improves EDA metrics.
source: Telles et al. (2013). Alternate nostril yoga breathing.

intervention: Hydrate and have a light snack if it has been more than 3 hours since eating. Blood sugar drops amplify stress.
evidence: Hypoglycemia activates the sympathetic nervous system and increases cortisol.
source: McEwen, B. S. (2007). Physiology and neurobiology of stress.

intervention: Open a window or step outside briefly. Fresh air and a brief change of environment can reset focus.
evidence: Environmental changes interrupt rumination cycles.
source: Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan, 1995).

intervention: Postpone non-urgent decisions for 30 minutes. Decision fatigue amplifies perceived stress.
evidence: Decision fatigue is linked to cortisol elevation (Hagger et al., 2010).
source: Hagger, M. S., et al. (2010). Ego depletion and the strength model of self-control.
